coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional nitrogen-doped carbon dots for HS- sensing and mitochondrial-targeted imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Haojie
Liu, Aikun
Zhang, Mingming
Song, Jun
Zeng, Pengju
Qu, Junle
Guo, Jiaqing
Li, Hao
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen sulfide ions (HS-) are an important endogenous ion species found in the human body, with HS- concentration changes known to play an important role in regulating various physiological activities. This study describes a simple strategy for preparing N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) using hydrothermal synthesis that utilize 3-aminophenol and tartaric acid. Aqueous solutions of these NCDs produced a green fluorescence under excitation at 494 nm and demonstrated good photostability under different NaCl concentrations, pH values and UV irradiation time. Moreover, the NCDs as probe also showed high sensitivity and selectivity for detecting HS-. Additionally, these NCDs exhibit excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity making them ideal for cell-based imaging and colocalization experiments that was further verified the ability of these NCDs to cross the cell membrane and label the mitochondria. More importantly, we can successfully realize the detection and visualization of exogenous HS- in mitochondria. Thus, these NCDs-based probe can reasonably be expected to replace common commercial probe especially in the organelle imaging and fluorescent visualization of the mitochondria.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent
HS- detection
Mitochondrial imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cellular imaging and mitochondrial-targeting using NCDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of the NCDs and their corresponding size distribution; (b) FT-IR spectrum of the NCDs; (c) XPS spectrum of the NCDs, and the corresponding C 1 s (d), N 1 s (e) and O 1 s (f) spectra.

(a) UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of the NCDs evaluated in this study. The inset images show representative pictures of an aqueous solution of these NCDs reacting to daylight and UV light, respectively. (b) 3D-fluorescence spectrum under different excitation wavelengths.

(a) Normalized fluorescence intensity of these NCDs at different NaCl concentrations; (b) Normalized fluorescence intensity of these NCDs under continuous UV irradiation; (c) Normalized fluorescence intensity of these NCDs in response to HS- over time; (d) Normalized fluorescence intensity of these NCDs at different pH values.
(a) NCD electivity in the presence of HS- or other ions (2 mM); (b) anti-interference of the NCDs toward HS- (2 mM) in the absence and presence of other ions (2 mM).

(a) Changes in the fluorescence emission spectrum for these NCDs in the presence of different HS- concentrations (0–50 μM); (b) Relationship between the concentration of HS- and the (F0-F)/F0 of the NCDs between 0 and 50 μM HS-, Inset: linear graph showing the relationship between signal and HS- concentration, between 0 and 10 μM; (c) Absorption spectrum for the NCDs, HS- solutions and NCDs+HS- solutions; (d) PL decay curves of the NCD solutions in response to different concentrations of HS- (from low to high: 0, 25, 50, and 75 μM) over time.
Cytotoxicity of different concentrations of these NCDs in Kyse-150 cells.

(a) Intracellular colocalization fluorescence imaging of our NCDs and Mito Tracker Red CMXRos in live Kyse-150 cells. Cells incubated with NCDs for 20 mins and Mito Tracker Red CMXRos for 30 mins at 37 ℃; (b) Colocalization scatter plots of the merged image; Pearson correlation coefficient: R= 0.952; (c) The normalized intensity profile of the regions within a straight line across cells in both the NCDs and Mito-Tracker images.

Confocal fluorescence images of HS- in Kyse-150 cells. The bright field (b, e), green fluorescence and (a, d) merged images (c, f) of Kyse-150 cells pretreated with 30 μL of NCD solution for 20 mins and then 50 μM HS- for another 5 mins. The cells were excited using a laser set to 488 nm.
Schematic describing the synthesis of the NCDs and their application in detecting HS-.
Comparison of detection limits for the determination of HS- by different fluorescent probes.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional nitrogen-doped carbon dots for HS- sensing and mitochondrial-targeted imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Photonics and Biophotonics, Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Photonics and Biophotonics, Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province, Shenzhen University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Biomedical Optics and Photonics (CBOP) & College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Key Lab of Optoelectronics Devices and systems of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Province, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Research Nuclear University MEPhI, Moscow Engineering Physics Institute, 115409 Moscow, Russia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Research Nuclear University MEPhI, Moscow Engineering Physics Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Research Nuclear University MEPhI (Moscow Engineering Physics Institute), 115409, Moscow, Russian Federation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogen sulfide ions (HS-) are an important endogenous ion species found in the human body, with HS- concentration changes known to play an important role in regulating various physiological activities. This study describes a simple strategy for preparing N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) using hydrothermal synthesis that utilize 3-aminophenol and tartaric acid. Aqueous solutions of these NCDs produced a green fluorescence under excitation at 494 nm and demonstrated good photostability under different NaCl concentrations, pH values and UV irradiation time. Moreover, the NCDs as probe also showed high sensitivity and selectivity for detecting HS-. Additionally, these NCDs exhibit excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity making them ideal for cell-based imaging and colocalization experiments that was further verified the ability of these NCDs to cross the cell membrane and label the mitochondria. More importantly, we can successfully realize the detection and visualization of exogenous HS- in mitochondria. Thus, these NCDs-based probe can reasonably be expected to replace common commercial probe especially in the organelle imaging and fluorescent visualization of the mitochondria.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen sulfide ions (HS-) exist in all aqueous solutions of hydrogen sulfide (H2S), which is a colorless, toxic, water-soluble gas best known for its rotten egg smell, and which, when used at high enough concentrations, can paralyze the olfactory nerve. However, recent studies have shown that it is also the third most common endogenous gas based signal in eukaryotes after carbon monoxide and nitric oxide. This ion is produced in both animal and plant tissues via specialized enzymes, and plays an important role in various physiological activities in these organisms [1–4]. Hydrogen sulfide can be found as the H2S molecule, hydrogen sulfide ion (HS-) and sulfur ion (S2-) in physiological systems with the proportion of each of these ions reaching a dynamic balance at physiological pH of about 30:70:000002 [5], making HS- the predominant species of hydrogen sulfide in these systems. This endogenous HS- is critical to the physiological function of most systems and is involved in various physiological reactions including central nervous regulation, vasodilation, alleviating myocardial injury, improving immune inflammation, and regulating hormone secretion [6–9]. In addition, several studies have shown that a change in the metabolic concentration of HS- in the brain can also be an indicator of both Down's syndrome [10] and Alzheimer's disease [11]. Therefore, accurate quantitative detection of HS- concentrations is of great significance, especially in the physiological and pathological evaluation of HS-, and may provide a relevant basis for early treatment and diagnosis of related diseases.

Given the importance of HS- in biomedicine, it is unsurprising that this ion has received significant attention in recent years, with several novel methods for its detection being developed over the last few decades, these include colorimetry [12], electrochemistry [13], Raman spectroscopy [14], molecular absorption spectroscopy [15], chromatography [16] and chemiluminescence [17]. However, although these methods all exhibit decent sensitivity and specificity, they are also usually accompanied by cumbersome sample processing, expensive instrumentation, high reagent costs and technically challenging operation, making the development of simpler alternatives an ongoing priority. Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) not only have the advantages of simple operation and preparation, low cost, and sustainability, they are also water soluble and exhibit excellent optical stability, while maintaining good sensitivity and low detection limits [18–20] making them a promising tool for fluorescent detection [21–23]. CDs also exhibit low cytotoxicity and good membrane permeability [24–26], making them suitable for application as fluorescent probes in biological imaging [27–30]. In recent years, fluorescence probe imaging technology based on CDs has been widely used in the sensing and imaging of specific subcellular organelle in vivo due to its advantages of high specificity, quick response, good biocompatibility and convenient synthesis. As we all know, mitochondria play an extremely important role in cell biology. It is an important place for eukaryotes to carry out oxidative metabolism and realize energy conversion. It is a major part of the production of the most direct energy source in organisms [31,32]. In addition, it also participates in important physiological processes such as signal molecule transduction, cell proliferation and apoptosis [32,33], most importantly, H2S producing enzymes are located in cytoplasm and mitochondria, where HS- can be produced [34]. Therefore, fluorescent probes targeting mitochondria can effectively achieve instantaneous tracking of metabolites and signal molecules involved in important physiological reactions by mitochondria, which is conducive to studying mitochondrial functions, and makes a significant contribution to the early diagnosis and later treatment of related diseases. In recent studies, a number of multifunctional mitochondrial fluorescent probes based on endogenous small molecule have been reported. They can not only locate in mitochondria but also detect various active small molecules in mitochondria, which successfully realize the detection and visualization of various active substances in mitochondria.

Here, we describe a novel method for creating simple, low-cost nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) via the application of hydrothermal synthesis using 3-aminophenol and tartaric acid as substrates. We then showed that these can be used as a probe to successfully detect HS- ( Scheme 1), with an aqueous solution of these NCDs emitting clear green fluorescence under ultraviolet light illumination. At the same time, the as-prepared NCDs shown the stable fluorescence properties. In addition, we demonstrated their specificity for HS- using a quenching assay, the results of which revealed a good linear relationship (0 ~ 10 μM) between the NCDs-based probe and ion and established the limit of detection for HS- at 105 nM, indicating that these probes were likely to facilitate the reliable visualization and selective detection of HS- in solution. More importantly, the NCDs as probe can also label the mitochondria of living cell. Based on these results, we can successfully realize the detection and visualization of exogenous HS- in mitochondria.

Both the 3-aminophenol and tartaric acid were supplied by Macklin (Shanghai, China) while the metal salts (NaHS, NaCl, KI, CaCl2, CuCl2, MgCl2, MnCl2, PbCl2, BaSO4, LiCl, CdCl2, CsBr, Na2SO3, Na2SO4, NaHCO3, AgNO3, KH2PO4, Na2HPO4, Na2CO3) were purchased from Shanghai Experiment Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China).

UV–vis absorption spectra were measured using a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu), while the fluorescence spectra were obtained by a Fluorolog Horiba spectrofluorometer. The surface morphology of the NCDs was evaluated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20) and the chemical structure of these compounds was identified by analyzing their Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectrum using a Nicolet 5700 FT-IR Spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corp, USA). The elemental composition and functional groups within these NCDs were identified using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi multifunctional imaging electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher).

A total of 0.3 g of 3-aminophenol and 0.1 g of tartaric acid were dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water, placed in a hydrothermal reactor within a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. Then, when the reactor had cooled to room temperature (25 °C), the supernatant was extracted and dialyzed using a 500 Da dialysis membrane to remove impurities, and the dialysis product was placed in fresh deionized water every 12 h for two days. The final white powder was then obtained by freeze-drying.

We used the Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) method to evaluate the cytotoxicity of these NCDs in Kyse-150 cells and the same cells were used in the imaging process. Kyse-150 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 37 °C in an incubator with 5% CO2 for 24 h and then used in either the viability or the fluorescence assays. For the fluorescence imaging these cells were placed in a confocal petri dish (1 mL medium) and then incubated with the NCDs (30 μL) in the cell culture incubator at 37 °C for 20 mins before being washed with PBS thrice and then immediately subjected to imaging in 1 mL of DMEM. All cell imaging was performed using a confocal laser-scanning microscope (CLSM) under a 60 × oil immersion lens at an excitation wavelength of 488 nm (The experiment was repeated at least 3 times). We then added a predetermined concentration of NaHS solution to these cells and evaluated ability of these NCDs to detect exogenous HS- in Kyse-150 cells.

We then validatde the localization ability of the NCDs to the mitochondria using Kyse-150 cells incubated with 30 μL NCDs at 37 °C for 20 mins and then 200 nM of a commercial mitochondrial probe (Mito Tracker Red CMXRos) for another 30 mins. These cells were then washed thrice in PBS and evaluated under CLSM. The excitation channels were set at 488 nm and 561 nm for the NCDs and Mito Tracker Red CMXRos, respectively.

We evaluated the surface morphology of the NCDs using TEM. These evaluations revealed that the NCDs appeared as randomly dispersed spheroid nanoparticles with a diameter distribution range of 2.00–7.16 nm ( Fig. 1a), and the average diameter was about 4.36 ± 1.21 nm. FT-IR spectrum was then used to identify the functional groups on the surface of the NCDs (Fig. 1b). These evaluations identified a wide absorption peak around 3447 cm−1, which is likely created by the stretching vibrations of N-H or -OH bonds, while the peak at 1630 cm−1 is attributed to the CO stretching vibration. The band in the 1492 cm−1 region was ascribed to CC stretching vibrations, and the other characteristics peaks at 1398 cm−1 and 1152 cm−1 are the result of stretching vibrations for C-N and C-O-C [35–38].

The chemical composition of the NCDs was further analyzed by XPS (Fig. 1c). This analysis identified three obvious peaks located at 284.8, 401.8 and 532.8 eV, representing C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. The C1 s peak (Fig. 1d) revealed three types of carbon bonds at 284.7 eV, 286.4 eV and 288.5 eV, representing C-C/CC, C-C/C-N and CO, respectively. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1e), was deconvoluted to three peaks at 399.7 eV, 400.5 eV and 401.9 eV, which represent C-NC, N-C and N-H, respectively. The O 1 s peak (Fig. 1f) was fitted against two peaks at 531.6 eV and 533.0 eV, which represent CO and C-O, respectively [39–43].

The optical properties of these NCDs can be described using their excitation, emission, and UV–vis absorption spectra. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (blue line; Fig. 2a) shows that these NCDs have two absorption peaks in the UV region, located at 235 nm and 280 nm, respectively. The first peak is likely a result of the π - π * transition of the CC bond, and the second peak is consistent with a n - π * transition in CO/CN [44–46]. An aqueous solution of these NCDs appeared yellow under a daylight lamp but emitted a green fluorescence when exposed to ultraviolet light (365 nm, inset in Fig. 2a). The excitation spectrum (black line) of the NCDs shows that their maximum excitation wavelength is 494 nm, and the emission spectrum (red line) revealed a maximum emission peak at 511 nm in response to excitation at 494 nm. Fig. 2b clearly illustrates the differences in the emission spectrum of these NCDs under different excitation conditions, suggesting that these NCDs present with typical excitation dependence characteristics, and their average emission wavelength is around 511 nm.

NCDs stability is critical for ion detection and cell imaging applications via its optical properties. Therefore, we went on to evaluate its optical stability using four criteria (1) NaCl ion concentration; (2) different luminescence time, (3) reaction time after adding quench agent, and (4) pH value. Our evaluations show that the fluorescence intensity of these NCDs did not change significantly when exposed to NaCl at concentrations of 0–200 μM ( Fig. 3a), indicating that these NCDs have good photostability in aqueous solutions with high NaCl concentrations. We then evaluated photobleaching in response to prolonged ultraviolet light exposure (Fig. 3b). We found that continuous irradiation at 365 nm for one hour had almost no effect on the signal intensity of these NCDs, suggesting that they have good optical stability. Fig. 3c reveals the relative speed of the HS- quenching response in these NCDs, revealing almost total inhibition within one min. In addition, these experiments also revealed that there was no signal recovery following HS- quenching, even when the NCDs were allowed to recover for over an hour. Our final evaluation revealed that fluorescence quenching occurred in response to a strong alkali environment, but that fluorescence intensity did not change significantly when the solution retained a pH of between 1 and 11, indicating that these NCDs are suitable for physiological experiments (Fig. 3d).

We then went on to evaluate our NCD specificity by evaluating signal quenching in response to mixtures of different ions, including the HS-, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Li+, Cd2+, Cs+, SO3 2-, SO4 2-, HCO3 -, NO3 -, HPO4 2-, H2PO4 -, HPO2 2-, I-, Cl-, Br- and CO3 2- (2 mM), and then measured their emission spectra ( Fig. 4a). These evaluations revealed that the fluorescence of the NCDs, as compared to the control (black bar), were only quenched after adding HS-, and remained fairly unchanged when mixed with any of the other ions, which indicated that these NCDs demonstrate good selectivity for HS-. Further experiments were then used to explore the selectivity of these NCDs and their sensitivity to any interfering factors (Fig. 4b). These experiments clearly demonstrated fluorescence quenching in response to HS-, and that there was no signal recovery in response to the addition of other interfering ions, indicating that these NCDs are fairly sensitive to and specific for HS-. Cysteine (Cys) and glutathione (GSH) as biological thiols widely exist in organisms. To further study the anti-interference of NCDs, as shown in Fig S1, the fluorescence is not significantly quenched after adding 2 mM Cys and GSH into NCDs solution, respectively, indicating that these two substances in organisms do not react with NCDs. Therefore, the NCDs have the potential to detect HS- in cell imaging.

The quenching effect of these NCDs at different concentrations of HS- was studied using titration, which allowed us to establish the detection limit and linear detection range of these constructs. These experiments revealed that the addition of HS- within 0–50 μM results in the gradual decrease in signal intensity ( Fig. 5a), and the fluorescence intensity of these NCDs was strongly linear when HS- were within the 0–10 μM range (Fig. 5b) producing a linear correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9979 (inset of Fig. 5b). This means that we were also able to determine the limit of detection for these constructs (105 nM) using the "triple standard deviation principle" [47]. As shown in Table 1, compared with other fluorescent probes reported previously for HS- detection, the NCDs has a lower detection limit and similar linear range. However, in recent years, a group called nitro-benz-2-oxa-1,3-diazole (NBD) was introduced into the surface of CDs through secondary modification to produce a novel carbon dot-based hybrid, which has a lower detection limit than the NCDs-based probe. However, it has more complex preparation methods, higher cost and poor photostability, and the introduction of new groups will reduce its biocompatibility in cell imaging. In conclusion, NCDs-based probe compared with other probes has great potential in the application of detecting HS-.

The application of NCDs to ion detection is based on the principle that the interactions between the analyte and the CDs reduces their fluorescence effect via quenching. These quenching mechanisms include inner filter effect (IFE), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), Foster resonance energy transfer (FRET), static quenching and dynamic quenching. Given this variety, we went on to determine the likely quenching mechanism underlying the interactions between these NCDs and the HS- ions [55–58]. The absorption spectrum of the NCDs hardly changed in response to the addition of HS- (Fig. 5c) indicating that no nonfluorescent complex was formed between these two components, eliminating static quenching and PET as potential mechanisms. Moreover, the absorption spectrum of the quench agent, HS-, does not overlap with the fluorescence spectrum of the NCDs, excluding both FRET and IFE. Following this, dynamic quenching remained, and we validated this relationship by evaluating the fluorescence lifetime of these NCDs. Fig. 5d shows the differences in NCDs fluorescence lifetime in response to different HS- concentrations (0–75 μM), with this lifetime decreasing significantly in response to increasing HS-. These results confirm that our observations were not the result of static quenching or IFE. Thus, taken together we can assume that this combination relies on dynamic quenching, where the excited NCDs are restored to ground state following an energy or charge transfer to the interacting ion following a collision between the quenching agent and NCDs.

In order to better understand the NCDs interacts with the HS-, the dynamic light scattering (DLS) and FT-IR of NCDs and NCDs with HS- (NCDs+HS-) was measured. As shown in Fig S2, the DLS of NCDs and NCDs+ HS-. For the NCDs, the diameter of NCDs+HS- slightly increased than the NCDs (from 4.36 to 16.34 nm). The experiment data indicate that the HS- can absorb the surface of NCDs. To understand how the HS- absorb the surface of NCDs, the FT-IR spectra of NCDs and NCDs+HS- was studied. The FT-IR spectra of NCDs and NCDs+HS- did not change significantly. They all showed absorption peaks at 3447 cm−1, 1630 cm−1 1492 cm−1 1398 cm−1 and 1152 cm−1, which correspond to N-H/-OH, CO, CC, C-N and C-O-C, respectively (Fig S3). It indicates that HS- is adsorbed on the surface of NCDs by non-covalent bond or hydrogen bond.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular imaging and mitochondrial-targeting using NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: These results suggest that NCDs may be suitable for application in cell imaging experiments. However, in order for them to be suitable for these applications we first had to establish their cytotoxicity ( Fig. 6). This was completed using Kyse-150 cells, which were incubated with different concentrations of the NCDs mixture and incubated for 24 h before being evaluated for cell viability using a CCK-8 assay. These assays revealed that cell viability remained above 90% even when the concentration of the NCDs was increased to 100 μg/mL, indicating that these NCDs had low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, making them an ideal candidate for living cell imaging.

Mitochondria are important organelles found in most cells, which act as the primary site for aerobic respiration. Mitochondrial staining is of great significance to the study of mitochondrial morphology and its role in cellular metabolism, growth, and apoptosis [33,59]. After staining Kyse-150 cells with the NCDs CLSM imaging revealed that the majority of the NCDs fluorescence was concentrated in the mitochondrial regions of the cells, illustrating the effective penetration of these constructs into the cell and their targeted staining of the mitochondria. This result suggests that these NCDs have an increased affinity for the mitochondria.

We then went on to validate this mitochondrial localization using a commercial mitochondrial probe, Mito Tracker Red CMXRos. Confocal imaging ( Fig. 7a) using green (488 nm) and red (561 nm) excitation channels for the NCDs and Mito Tracker Red CMXRos, respectively allowed us to investigate the colocalization of these signals within the Kyse-150 cells used throughout this study. These images revealed a colocalization coincidence rate of over 95.0% (Fig. 7b), and the normalized intensity profiles of the two channels along the straight segment show a significant degree of coincidence between these probes in Kyse-150 cells (Fig. 7c). To further investigate how NCDs retain in mitochondria, as shown in Fig S4, the positive Zeta potential of NCDs was 4.27 ± 0.245 mV, while the mitochondrial membrane potential of cells is a high negative potential [60]. Therefore, the NCDs with positive charge could accumulate on mitochondria of living cells through electrostatic action. These results suggest that our NCDs exhibit a significant affinity for the mitochondria in Kyse-150 cells and when combined with the above experiments, our results indicated that these NCDs exhibit good biocompatibility, low cost production, ease of use and preparation and the application of longer wavelengths making them a likely candidate for developing novel commercial mitochondrial probes.

To verify whether the fluorescence of mitochondria would reproduce after being quenched. We added HS- to living cells to quench the fluorescence of mitochondria, and then continued to observe the fluorescence changes for a period of time. As shown in Fig S5, before the addition of HS-, the mitochondria of Kyse-150 cells stained by NCDs showed green fluorescence. After the addition of 50 μM HS- for 5 min, the green fluorescence intensity was quenched. Then, since the fluorescence of mitochondria was not recovered after 15 min, the experimental data indicate that the fluorescence of the mitochondria could not reproduce after quenching by HS-. Therefore, it is possible for the probe to be used for biosensor detection of HS-. Fig. 8 shows the CLSM images of the Kyse-150 cells incubated with 30 μL of our NCDs for 20 mins and then 50 μM HS- for 5 mins at 37 °C. These images clearly reveal that the mitochondria in the cytoplasm of Kyse-150 cells incubated with the NCDs exhibit bright fluorescence, and that the addition of 50 μM HS- results in a significant reduction in fluorescent signal, indicating that HS- penetrates the cell membrane and is absorbed by the mitochondria quenching the NCDs signal. Considering that the quenching effect of mitochondrial fluorescence with different gradient HS- concentration, Fig. S6 shows the CLSM images of NCDs (30 μL) treated Kyse-150 cells for 20 mins and then four kinds of concentrations of HS- added into NCDs (30 μL) treated Kyse-150 cells for 5 mins at 37 ℃. The fluorescence intensity of mitochondria decreases with increasing HS- concentration. In addition, we suggest that the reason that these NCDs demonstrate such a high affinity for the mitochondria is that these organelles are the primary site of H2S production in the cells [61], which allows the NCDs to react with their HS- ions. As the endogenous H2S within the mitochondria is consumed, so exogenous NaHS is preferentially supplied to mitochondria to act as a donor in the respiratory pathway [34,62]. Therefore, fluorescence quenching primarily occurs in or around the mitochondria during cell imaging. Thus, this type of imaging could provide a reference for studying the activity of HS- in mitochondria [63,64], and indicates that these NCDs have potential applications in detecting exogenous HS- in mitochondria of living cells.

In summary, we were able to successfully prepare nitrogen doped mitochondrial targeting carbon dots (NCDs) using the hydrothermal method using 3-aminophenol and tartaric acid as precursors. These NCDs demonstrate good water solubility, excellent optical stability and high sensitivity and selectivity for HS-, and when compared with other probes for HS- detection, these NCDs-based probe have the advantages of low cost, sustainability, reduced response time, low detection limits (105 nM) and high detection accuracy (0–10 μM), which make this an excellent potential probe for evaluating HS-. In addition, these NCDs also demonstrated good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, and remarkable specificity for the mitochondria, with colocalization experiments in Kyse-150 cells demonstrating that these NCDs had a high rate of colocalization with the commercial probe, further supporting their further development as commercial probes for mitochondrial research. More importantly, we can successfully realize the detection and visualization of exogenous HS- in mitochondria. This paper provides a novel approach for developing carbon dots designed to detect metabolically important substances at the organelle level, and may provide a new tool for identifying important information for the early diagnosis of related diseases in the future.

Haojie Cai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Aikun Liu: Methodology, Investigation. Mingming Zhang: Validation, Resources. Jun Song: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Pengju Zeng: Investigation, Resources. Junle Qu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Jiaqing Guo: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Hao Li: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (61620106016 / 61835009 / 62175161 / 61775145); China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2021M702240); Shenzhen Basic Research Program (JCYJ20210324095810028 / JCYJ20210324095613036). We thank Photonics Research Center of Shenzhen University and Ph.D. Lin for the technical assistance with the Leica SP8 confocal microscopy instrument.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132048.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The possible role of hydrogen sulfide as an endogenous neuromodulator

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.J. Polhemus, D.J. Lefer, Emergence of hydrogen sulfide as an endogenous gaseous signaling molecule in cardiovascular disease, Circ Res, 114(2014) 730–737, https://doi.org/10.1161/CIRCRESAHA.114.300505.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gasotransmitters and the immune system: mode of action and novel therapeutic targets

Tan, DNA-Capped Silver Nanoflakes as Fluorescent Nanosensor for Highly Sensitive Imaging of Endogenous H2S in Cell Division Cycles, Analytical Chemistry, 91(2019) 15404–15410, https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.analchem.9b02527.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Abate, A. Festa, M. Falco, A. Lombardi, A. Luce, A. Grimaldi, et al., Mitochondria as playmakers of apoptosis, autophagy and senescence, Semin Cell Dev Biol, 98(2020) 139–153, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.semcdb.2019.05.022.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Guo, Y. Sun, J. Li, X. Geng, R. Yang, X. Zhang, et al., Fluorescent Carbon Dots Shuttling between Mitochondria and the Nucleolus for in Situ Visualization of Cell Viability, ACS Applied Bio Materials, 4(2020) 928–934, https://doi.org/10.1021/acsabm.0c01408.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Bdp, C. Shsab, E. Kkd, Effects of hydrogen sulfide on mitochondrial function and cellular bioenergetics, Redox Biology, (2020) 101772, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.redox.2020.101772.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Luo, Z. Tang, J. Yu, W. Liao, M.M. Dawuda, Hydrogen sulfide negatively regulates cd-induced cell death in cucumber (Cucumis sativus L) root tip cells, BMC Plant Biology, 20(2020) 1–13, https://doi.org/10.1186/s12870–020-02687–8.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Chen, C. Zhu, Z. Yang, J. Chen, Y. He, Y. Jiao, et al., A ratiometric fluorescent probe for rapid detection of hydrogen sulfide in mitochondria, Angew Chem Int Ed Engl, 52(2013) 1688–1691, https://doi.org/10.1002/anie.201207701.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of hydrogen sulfide on mitochondrial function and cellular bioenergetics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.D. Paul, S.H. Snyder, K. Kashfi, Effects of hydrogen sulfide on mitochondrial function and cellular bioenergetics, Redox Biol, 38(2021) 101772, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.redox.2020.101772.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Haojie Cai was born in Guangdong, China, in 1997. He received the B.S. degree from Shenzhen University, Guangdong, China, in 2020. Since 2020, he has been a master at Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, China.

Aikun Liu was born in Shandong, China, in 1993. She received the B.S. degree from Taishan Medical University, Shandong province, China, in 2016. And the M.S. degrees from Shandong First Medical University, Shandong province, China, in 2019, respectively. Since 2019, she has been a PHD at Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, China.

Mingming Zhang was born in Henan, China, in 1997. He received the B.S. degree from Henan Polytechnic University, China, in 2020. Since September 2020, he has been a master at Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, China.

Jun Song was born in Inner Mongolia, China, in August 1978. He received the B. Eng. degree in information engineering from Zhejiang University, Zhejiang, China, in 2001, and the Ph.D. degrees from Zhejiang University, Hangzhou, China, in 2006. Since 2017, he has been a professor with the Institute of Optoelectronics, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, China. His research interests include nanophotonics and its applications in renewable energy, biomedicine, electronic information, and environmental protection.

Pengju Zeng was born in Jiangxi, China, in 1960. He received the B.S. degree from Jiangxi Normal University, Nanchang, China, in 1983. And the M.S degree from Nanjing University, Nanjing, China, in 1992. In 2001, he has been a professor. Since 2002, he works in the Institute of Optoelectronics, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, China. His research interests include organic semiconductor synthesis technology, optical properties of organic semiconductors and organic semiconductor device.

Junle Qu was born in Shanxi, China, in 1970. He received the B.S. degree from Xi’ an Jiao Tong University, Xi’ an, China, in 1992, and the Ph.D. degrees from the Xi’ an Institute of Optics and FineMechanics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, China, in 1998, respectively. Since 2003, he has been a Professor with the Institute of Optoelectronics, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, China. His research interests include nanophotonics, biophotonics, microscopic imaging technique, and laser treatment technology.

Jiaqing Guo was born in Shanxi，China, in 1992. He received the B.S. and the M.S. degree from Minnan Normal University, Fujian, China, in 2015 and 2018, and the Ph.D. degrees from Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, China, in 2020. Since 2020, he has been a Post-doctor at Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, China.

Hao Li was born in Jilin, China, in 1985. He received the B.S. and the M.S. degree from Changchun Science and Engineering University, Changchun, China, in 2008 and 2011, and the Ph.D. degrees from Soochow University, Suzhou, China, in 2016. Since 2020, he has been a associate researcher at Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, China. His research interests include preparation of carbon dots and their application in bioimaging and optoelectronic devices.
